headline: UK gilt yields pushed to multi-year highs as fiscal and political uncertainty gripped the market.
bullets: Long-dated gilts sold off again: the 20-year yield rose about 8bp on the day to 5.51% and the 10-year climbed to ~5.02% — near its highest since 2008 and the biggest weekly jump in roughly two months.

The main driver was UK fiscal and political uncertainty — speculation around a Labour leadership challenge to PM Keir Starmer and doubts over the government's "fiscal flexibility" pushed UK borrowing costs to a G7 high.
Firmer oil prices added to inflation worries and trimmed expectations for near-term Bank of England rate cuts (base rate 4.00%), reinforcing the upward pressure on yields.
Softer demand at recent gilt auctions (bid-to-cover near 3.4) signalled cooler appetite for UK debt, even as the FTSE 100 rose ~1.9% and sterling held around $1.34.
For DB pension schemes this is supportive for funding: higher long-dated yields reduce the present value of liabilities (see the estimated impact above).
